content.text: Logging and Telemetry Standards for GenAI Applications

GenAI applications have unique observability requirements that go beyond standard web service telemetry. Token usage drives cost directly, model responses need to be auditable without storing raw user content, and the latency distribution of LLM calls (time-to-first-token vs. total generation time) is different from typical database queries. Without a deliberate telemetry standard, teams end up with inconsistent metrics that cannot be aggregated across services, log records that contain PII, and no way to answer 'why did our AI costs spike this week?'.

This document defines the three-signal telemetry model for GenAI applications (traces, metrics, structured logs), specifies the mandatory field set for each, and provides implementation patterns using OpenTelemetry and Python's standard logging.

attrs.alt: GenAI telemetry signal architecture — traces, metrics, and structured logs feed into Grafana

content.text: The three pillars of observability (traces, metrics, logs) each serve a different question. For GenAI applications, the specific answers each signal provides are:
Where did the time go in this request?
Which span is the LLM call? Which span is embedding retrieval? What model and token count?
Is the system healthy right now?
Are token costs within budget? Is cache hit rate declining? Is p99 latency breaching SLO?
What happened in a specific request?
What was the finish_reason? Was the response truncated? Which prompt variant was used?

Every LLM API call must be wrapped in an OpenTelemetry span. The span captures timing, the model used, token counts, and the finish reason. Spans are nested under the parent request span so the full call tree is visible.

from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.trace import Span
from src.services.genai.interface import ChatMessage, ChatResult

tracer = trace.get_tracer("genai.service", "1.0.0")

# Standard span attribute names — consistent naming enables cross-service aggregation
ATTR_LLM_PROVIDER       = "llm.provider"
ATTR_LLM_MODEL          = "llm.model"
ATTR_LLM_INPUT_TOKENS   = "llm.tokens.input"
ATTR_LLM_OUTPUT_TOKENS  = "llm.tokens.output"
ATTR_LLM_TOTAL_TOKENS   = "llm.tokens.total"
ATTR_LLM_FINISH_REASON  = "llm.finish_reason"
ATTR_LLM_COST_USD       = "llm.cost_usd"
ATTR_LLM_CACHE_HIT      = "llm.cache_hit"
ATTR_LLM_REQUEST_ID     = "llm.request_id"

TOKEN_COST_USD: dict[str, dict[str, float]] = {
    "claude-sonnet-4-6":        {"input": 3.00e-6, "output": 15.0e-6},
    "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001": {"input": 0.25e-6, "output": 1.25e-6},
    "gpt-4o-2024-11-20":        {"input": 2.50e-6, "output": 10.0e-6},
    "gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18":   {"input": 0.15e-6, "output": 0.60e-6},
}

async def traced_chat(
    client,   # LLMClient adapter
    messages: list[ChatMessage],
    *,
    model: str,
    provider: str,
    system: str | None = None,
    max_tokens: int = 1024,
) -> ChatResult:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("llm.chat") as span:
        span.set_attribute(ATTR_LLM_PROVIDER, provider)
        span.set_attribute(ATTR_LLM_MODEL, model)

        result = await client.chat(
            messages, system=system, max_tokens=max_tokens
        )

        span.set_attribute(ATTR_LLM_INPUT_TOKENS,  result.input_tokens)
        span.set_attribute(ATTR_LLM_OUTPUT_TOKENS, result.output_tokens)
        span.set_attribute(ATTR_LLM_TOTAL_TOKENS,  result.input_tokens + result.output_tokens)
        span.set_attribute(ATTR_LLM_FINISH_REASON, result.finish_reason)

        costs = TOKEN_COST_USD.get(model, {})
        if costs:
            cost = result.input_tokens * costs["input"] + result.output_tokens * costs["output"]
            span.set_attribute(ATTR_LLM_COST_USD, round(cost, 8))

        return result
Span name: 'llm.chat', 'llm.embed', 'llm.stream_chat' — verb.noun format, no model name in the span name (model is an attribute).
Always set span status to ERROR on exception: span.set_status(trace.StatusCode.ERROR, str(exc)).
Child spans for RAG: create a 'rag.retrieve' child span with attributes 'rag.collection', 'rag.k', 'rag.query_tokens'.
Never put prompt content or response text in span attributes — attributes are indexed and stored long-term; they must not contain user data.

Metrics are pre-aggregated numeric measurements suitable for dashboards and alerting. The mandatory metric set for a GenAI service covers token throughput, cost, latency, error rate, and cache effectiveness.

from opentelemetry import metrics

meter = metrics.get_meter("genai.service", "1.0.0")

# Counters — monotonically increasing
input_tokens_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "llm.tokens.input",
    unit="tokens",
    description="Total input tokens sent to LLM providers",
)
output_tokens_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "llm.tokens.output",
    unit="tokens",
    description="Total output tokens received from LLM providers",
)
cost_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "llm.cost_usd",
    unit="USD",
    description="Cumulative estimated cost of LLM API calls",
)
error_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "llm.errors",
    description="Count of LLM API errors by provider, model, and status",
)
cache_hit_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "llm.cache_hits",
    description="Semantic cache hits (avoids LLM call)",
)

# Histograms — for percentile computation (p50/p95/p99)
latency_histogram = meter.create_histogram(
    "llm.latency_ms",
    unit="ms",
    description="End-to-end LLM call latency in milliseconds",
)
ttft_histogram = meter.create_histogram(
    "llm.ttft_ms",
    unit="ms",
    description="Time to first token for streaming calls",
)

def record_llm_call(
    result,
    *,
    provider: str,
    model: str,
    feature: str,   # e.g. "chat", "summarise", "classify" — enables per-feature cost breakdown
    latency_ms: float,
    cache_hit: bool = False,
) -> None:
    labels = {"provider": provider, "model": model, "feature": feature}
    input_tokens_counter.add(result.input_tokens, labels)
    output_tokens_counter.add(result.output_tokens, labels)
    latency_histogram.record(latency_ms, labels)
    if cache_hit:
        cache_hit_counter.add(1, labels)
    costs = TOKEN_COST_USD.get(model, {})
    if costs:
        cost = result.input_tokens * costs["input"] + result.output_tokens * costs["output"]
        cost_counter.add(cost, {"provider": provider, "model": model, "feature": feature})

def record_llm_error(*, provider: str, model: str, status: int | None, feature: str) -> None:
    error_counter.add(1, {
        "provider": provider,
        "model": model,
        "feature": feature,
        "status": str(status or "connection"),
    })

Structured logs capture per-request detail that metrics cannot — the specific finish reason, the prompt variant used, whether output was truncated. All log records must be JSON lines emitted to stdout. They must never contain raw prompt text or response text; instead log a SHA-256 hash of the content for correlation without storing PII.

import hashlib
import json
import logging
import time
from typing import Any

# Configure root logger for JSON output
import sys
root_logger = logging.getLogger()
handler = logging.StreamHandler(sys.stdout)
handler.setFormatter(logging.Formatter("%(message)s"))
root_logger.addHandler(handler)
root_logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)

logger = logging.getLogger("genai.request")

def _sha256_prefix(text: str, length: int = 8) -> str:
    return hashlib.sha256(text.encode()).hexdigest()[:length]

def log_llm_request(
    *,
    trace_id: str,
    span_id: str,
    provider: str,
    model: str,
    feature: str,
    input_tokens: int,
    output_tokens: int,
    finish_reason: str,
    latency_ms: float,
    cache_hit: bool,
    cost_usd: float,
    prompt_hash: str,          # sha256[:8] of the full prompt string
    response_hash: str,        # sha256[:8] of the response text
    user_id: str | None,       # hashed or tokenised user identifier; never raw email
    extra: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
) -> None:
    record = {
        "timestamp": time.strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ", time.gmtime()),
        "level": "INFO",
        "logger": "genai.request",
        "trace_id": trace_id,
        "span_id": span_id,
        "provider": provider,
        "model": model,
        "feature": feature,
        "input_tokens": input_tokens,
        "output_tokens": output_tokens,
        "finish_reason": finish_reason,
        "latency_ms": round(latency_ms, 2),
        "cache_hit": cache_hit,
        "cost_usd": round(cost_usd, 8),
        "prompt_hash": prompt_hash,
        "response_hash": response_hash,
        "user_id": user_id,
        **(extra or {}),
    }
    print(json.dumps(record), flush=True)

# Usage in the service layer:
# log_llm_request(
#     trace_id=span.get_span_context().trace_id,
#     span_id=span.get_span_context().span_id,
#     prompt_hash=_sha256_prefix(assembled_prompt),
#     response_hash=_sha256_prefix(result.content),
#     user_id=hashlib.sha256(user_email.encode()).hexdigest()[:16],
#     ...
# )

Never log raw prompt or response text at INFO or ERROR level. If your team needs prompt content for debugging, log it at DEBUG level behind a feature flag that is disabled in production. A DEBUG log line containing a user's query is a GDPR/CCPA data processing record that triggers retention and access control obligations.
Audit Log — Separate from Application Logs

The audit log is not the application log. It is a compliance artefact that records who invoked which AI feature, when, and with what inputs (hashed). It must be append-only, retained for the compliance period (typically 7 years for SOC 2), and stored in a separate storage system from the application log.

import hashlib, json, time
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict

@dataclass
class AuditRecord:
    event_id: str             # UUID
    event_type: str           # "llm.request" | "tool.call" | "rag.retrieve"
    timestamp_utc: str
    user_id_hash: str         # sha256(user_id)
    tenant_id: str
    feature: str
    model: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    finish_reason: str
    prompt_hash: str          # sha256(prompt)[:16]
    response_hash: str        # sha256(response)[:16]
    prev_hash: str            # hash of previous record — chain integrity
    record_hash: str          # sha256(this record minus record_hash)

    @classmethod
    def create(
        cls,
        *,
        event_id: str,
        event_type: str,
        user_id: str,
        tenant_id: str,
        feature: str,
        model: str,
        input_tokens: int,
        output_tokens: int,
        finish_reason: str,
        prompt: str,
        response: str,
        prev_hash: str,
    ) -> "AuditRecord":
        record = cls(
            event_id=event_id,
            event_type=event_type,
            timestamp_utc=time.strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ", time.gmtime()),
            user_id_hash=hashlib.sha256(user_id.encode()).hexdigest(),
            tenant_id=tenant_id,
            feature=feature,
            model=model,
            input_tokens=input_tokens,
            output_tokens=output_tokens,
            finish_reason=finish_reason,
            prompt_hash=hashlib.sha256(prompt.encode()).hexdigest()[:16],
            response_hash=hashlib.sha256(response.encode()).hexdigest()[:16],
            prev_hash=prev_hash,
            record_hash="",
        )
        payload = json.dumps({k: v for k, v in asdict(record).items() if k != "record_hash"}, sort_keys=True)
        record.record_hash = hashlib.sha256(payload.encode()).hexdigest()
        return record
Every GenAI service should ship a Grafana dashboard as code (JSON model in the repository). The following panels are mandatory; teams may add feature-specific panels on top.
Three signals — traces, metrics, structured logs — each answer a different observability question. All three are required for a production GenAI service.

Every LLM API call gets an OpenTelemetry span with standardised attribute names: llm.provider, llm.model, llm.tokens.input/output, llm.finish_reason, llm.cost_usd. Never put prompt content in span attributes.

Five mandatory metrics cover token throughput (counters), cost (counter), latency (histogram), errors (counter), and cache hits (counter). Alerts on error rate, p99 latency, cost spike, and token quota burn.

Structured log records are JSON lines with a fixed field set including trace_id correlation, prompt_hash (SHA-256 prefix), response_hash, and hashed user_id. Raw prompt and response text are never logged at INFO or ERROR level.
The audit log is separate from the application log: append-only, hash-chained, retained for the compliance period, stored in a dedicated system.

A Grafana dashboard JSON model ships alongside the service code with six mandatory panels: token throughput, cost rate, p99 latency, error rate by provider, cache hit rate, and finish reason breakdown.
